topic: Theme F: Religion, Human Rights & Social Justice
subject: religious-studies

items.q: What do orthodox Christians believe about the nature of Jesus?
items.correct: He is fully God and fully human at the same time (the incarnation) — both natures united in one person, as defined at the Council of Chalcedon.
items.misconception: Jesus was half God and half human.
items.random_wrong: Jesus was a human who became God after his resurrection.
items.why: The 'half and half' model was historically rejected as heresy. Orthodox doctrine holds both natures fully and simultaneously — a deliberately paradoxical claim students often soften.

items.q: What does karma mean in Hindu and Buddhist thought?
items.correct: The principle that actions have moral consequences shaping future experience or rebirth — not fate, but ethical cause and effect built into reality.
items.misconception: Karma is when bad things happen to bad people as punishment.
items.random_wrong: Karma is a god who decides what happens to you.
items.why: Students treat karma as cosmic justice meted out by a deity. In its philosophical origins it is impersonal moral causation — your own actions, not external judgement, shape your circumstances.

items.q: What is the difference between act and rule utilitarianism?
items.correct: Act utilitarianism judges each individual action by its consequences; rule utilitarianism follows rules that, if generally adopted, would produce the most good — sacrificing case-by-case calculation for predictability and fairness.
items.misconception: Act and rule utilitarianism are basically the same approach.
items.random_wrong: Act utilitarianism is about action movies and rule utilitarianism is about laws.
items.why: The distinction matters for famous counterexamples (e.g. punishing the innocent). Act utilitarianism might endorse it if outcomes are better; rule utilitarianism rejects such practices because the rule would be harmful overall.
It's 1948. “1945” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Gutierrez. “Bonhoeffer” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many articles in the UDHR?
items.why: Exact answer: 30 articles. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
